canonical_definition: LooGLE is consistently described across the provided literature as a benchmark or workload designed to evaluate large language models. The primary application of this instrument is to measure "Long-context processing," with definitions framing it as a tool for assessing both "long document question answering" and "long-context understanding." A defining feature highlighted in the sources is its use of prompts containing extensive text. As one paper notes, it "features extremely long documents... with over 24,000 tokens each" to assess both short- and long-dependency tasks ("Agri-CM3: AChinese Massive Multi-modal, Multi-level Benchmark for Agricultural Understanding and Reasoning"). In practice, it is cited as a workload alongside others for comparative system evaluations. The available data presents a unified view of LooGLE as an instrument for assessing an LLM's ability to derive answers from very long textual inputs.
papers: Agri-CM3: AChinese Massive Multi-modal, Multi-level Benchmark for Agricultural Understanding and Reasoning
MadaKV: Adaptive Modality-PerceptionKVCache Eviction for Efficient Multimodal Long-Context Inference
Bridging Context Gaps: Leveraging Coreference Resolution for Long Contextual Understanding
Not All Heads Matter: A Head-Level KV Cache Compression Method with Integrated Retrieval and Reasoning
Preble: Efficient Distributed Prompt Scheduling for LLM Serving

definitions.text: A workload for evaluating long document question answering, featuring prompts containing extensive text from which answers must be derived.
definitions.paper: Preble: Efficient Distributed Prompt Scheduling for LLM Serving

relationships.paper: Not All Heads Matter: A Head-Level KV Cache Compression Method with Integrated Retrieval and Reasoning
